heroOverview: Citrullinemia (ICD-10: E72.23) refers to two distinct inherited disorders in which the amino acid citrulline accumulates to toxic levels in the blood. Citrullinemia type I (CTLN1) — classic citrullinemia — results from deficiency of argininosuccinate synthetase 1 (ASS1), an enzyme in the urea cycle, and causes life-threatening hyperammonemia in the newborn period without treatment. Citrullinemia type II (CTLN2) results from citrin deficiency, a mitochondrial aspartate-glutamate carrier defect, and presents differently: in infancy with neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis, or in adulthood with episodic hyperammonemia and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Classic citrullinemia type I affects approximately 1 in 57,000 births in some screened populations and, without emergency management, causes neonatal coma and death. With newborn screening and nitrogen scavenger therapy, outcomes are substantially better, though the long-term neurological burden remains a challenge for many patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Citrullinemia type I: approximately 1 in 57,000 births (Asian populations have higher rates of type II); combined global estimate 1 in 57,000–250,000 live births
keyStats.demographics: Autosomal recessive for both types; type I presents in the neonatal period; type II has two distinct presentations — neonatal cholestasis in infancy or adult-onset hyperammonemia typically in East Asian adults
keyStats.avgAge: Type I: symptoms within 24–72 hours of birth; Type II adult form: typically ages 20–50 years at first neuropsychiatric episode
keyStats.globalCases: CTLN1 is pan-ethnic; CTLN2 caused by SLC25A13 mutations is concentrated in East Asian populations, particularly Japan (~1 in 17,000) and China

definition: Citrullinemia encompasses two genetically and biochemically distinct disorders, both resulting in plasma citrulline elevation. Citrullinemia type I (CTLN1, ICD-10: E72.23) is caused by biallelic mutations in ASS1 on chromosome 9q34, encoding argininosuccinate synthetase 1, the enzyme that condenses citrulline and aspartate to form argininosuccinate in step 3 of the urea cycle. Without ASS1, the urea cycle halts at citrulline; ammonia accumulates rapidly because the cycle cannot proceed, and citrulline itself rises to 20–50 times normal in severe neonatal cases. Plasma ammonia in untreated neonates typically exceeds 1,000–2,000 μmol/L (normal < 50 μmol/L in neonates) within the first days of life. Citrullinemia type II (CTLN2) is caused by biallelic mutations in SLC25A13, encoding citrin, a calcium-binding aspartate-glutamate carrier on the inner mitochondrial membrane. Citrin deficiency disrupts the malate-aspartate shuttle in hepatocytes, impairing both the urea cycle and the cytosolic NADH redox balance. The disorder manifests in infancy as neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency (NICCD) and in adulthood as citrullinemia type II, with episodic hyperammonemia and hepatic encephalopathy. Both forms fall under metabolic medicine and endocrinology; hepatology and neurology are frequently involved.

typesClassification.type: Citrullinemia type I (CTLN1) — neonatal severe form
typesClassification.description: Complete or near-complete ASS1 deficiency causing hyperammonemic crisis within 24–72 hours of birth. Without immediate treatment the neonate progresses from poor feeding and lethargy to coma and death within days.
Partial ASS1 activity causes milder or later-presenting hyperammonemia. May present in childhood or adulthood with episodic confusion, headaches, or vomiting rather than neonatal crisis.

Citrin (SLC25A13) deficiency presenting in adulthood with episodic hyperammonemia, cognitive symptoms, and sometimes cirrhosis. Characteristic food preferences (preference for protein-rich and lipid-rich foods, aversion to carbohydrate-heavy meals) are reported.

typesClassification.type: Neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency (NICCD)
typesClassification.description: The neonatal/infantile form of citrin deficiency, presenting with cholestatic jaundice, liver dysfunction, and galactosuria in infancy. Most patients recover liver function spontaneously by age 1 year but may progress to CTLN2 in adulthood.

primarySymptoms: Hyperammonemic encephalopathy in the first 24–72 hours of life (type I neonatal): rapid progression from poor feeding, lethargy, and hypotonia to respiratory alkalosis, seizures, and coma as ammonia rises above 1,000 μmol/L.
Persistent vomiting and refusal to feed in a newborn, often the earliest parental observation before frank neurological deterioration.
Hypotonia and abnormal tone in the neonatal period, including opisthotonus (severe backward arching) in fully decompensated infants.
Seizures in the newborn or during acute decompensation episodes in older patients, driven by acute ammonia neurotoxicity.
Episodic confusion, disorientation, abnormal behaviour, or aggression in children and adults with type I or type II disease during partial metabolic decompensation.
Neuropsychiatric symptoms in adults with type II: mood changes, memory impairment, episodes of mania, delusions, or aggression that often precede the formal diagnosis.
Liver disease (hepatomegaly, elevated transaminases, coagulopathy) in neonates with NICCD and in adults with long-standing CTLN2 with cirrhosis.

Unusual food preferences in adults with CTLN2: strong craving for protein-rich foods (meat, fish, eggs, tofu) and aversion to carbohydrate-heavy foods, reflecting the metabolic demand for aspartate generated from dietary protein.
Headache, lethargy, and impaired concentration during mild hyperammonemia episodes, often triggered by a high-carbohydrate meal in CTLN2 patients.
Growth failure and delayed development in children with poorly controlled type I disease.
earlyWarningSigns: A neonate who feeds poorly and becomes abnormally sleepy within the first 48 hours of life, particularly after protein feeding begins.
Mild unexplained elevation in plasma ammonia on a neonatal screen or during a routine admission for jaundice.
An adult of East Asian descent who develops episodes of confusion or unusual behaviour after high-carbohydrate meals.
Persistent neonatal jaundice with elevated direct bilirubin and fatty liver beyond four weeks of age in a child from a high-prevalence region (NICCD).
emergencySigns: Plasma ammonia above 200 μmol/L in any neonate, or above 150 μmol/L in an older child or adult — a metabolic emergency requiring immediate hospitalisation.
Rapid neurological deterioration: seizures, loss of consciousness, or decerebrate posturing in a neonate or child with known or suspected citrullinemia.
Acute liver failure signs — jaundice with coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and hypoglycaemia — in an infant or adult with known citrin deficiency.
Sudden psychiatric decompensation with violence, psychosis, or complete disorientation in an adult with known or suspected CTLN2.

causes.description: Over 80 pathogenic mutations in ASS1 on chromosome 9q34 have been reported, ranging from complete null mutations in the severe neonatal form to partial loss-of-function mutations in attenuated cases. Ammonia accumulates because citrulline cannot be condensed with aspartate to complete the urea cycle, halting nitrogen disposal.

Mutations in the citrin gene disrupt the malate-aspartate shuttle in hepatocyte mitochondria. Cytosolic NADH cannot be re-oxidised, impairing gluconeogenesis and aspartate availability for the urea cycle. This dual defect produces both liver disease and hyperammonemia. Founder mutations in East Asian populations — particularly 851del4 in Japan — account for the geographic concentration.

ASS1 deficiency blocks step 3 of the five-step urea cycle between citrulline and argininosuccinate. Proximal intermediates (ammonia, glutamine, citrulline) accumulate while distal products (argininosuccinate, arginine, urea) are deficient. Arginine becomes conditionally essential because it cannot be synthesised from citrulline.

Citrin deficiency causes accumulation of cytosolic NADH in hepatocytes, which inhibits gluconeogenesis and drives lactic acidosis. The resulting energy deficit in the liver impairs multiple metabolic pathways simultaneously, explaining why CTLN2 patients develop both metabolic and liver disease.

Because arginine is normally produced further along the urea cycle from citrulline, CTLN1 patients have relative arginine deficiency; this impairs protein synthesis, immune function, and nitric oxide production, requiring oral arginine supplementation as part of treatment.

riskFactors.factor: Biallelic ASS1 or SLC25A13 mutations (autosomal recessive)
riskFactors.description: Both parents must carry one defective allele. Each child has a 25% chance of inheriting both and being affected.

Founder mutations in SLC25A13 are concentrated in Japan, China, Korea, and other East Asian populations. In Japan, carrier frequency for the 851del4 mutation is approximately 1 in 65, making CTLN2 relatively common there.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of newborn screening for ASS1 deficiency
riskFactors.description: Countries without expanded newborn screening that measures citrulline by tandem mass spectrometry do not detect CTLN1 before the neonatal hyperammonemic crisis; delayed diagnosis significantly worsens neurological outcomes.
Carbohydrate-heavy meals worsen the cytosolic NADH imbalance in citrin deficiency, precipitating hyperammonemia. CTLN2 patients are advised to favour protein and fat over carbohydrate.
Fever, surgery, prolonged fasting, or any condition increasing endogenous protein catabolism can trigger acute hyperammonemia in both type I and type II citrullinemia.
Related couples have a higher probability of carrying the same citrullinemia-causing allele, raising the incidence of affected offspring in any ethnic group.

affectedDemographics: Neonates of any ethnicity with type I citrullinemia (ASS1 deficiency)
East Asian infants and adults with type II citrullinemia (citrin/SLC25A13 deficiency) — particularly Japanese and Chinese populations
Equal sex distribution for both forms
Infants with NICCD typically of East Asian descent presenting with neonatal cholestasis
Adults aged 20–50 years who present for the first time with episodic neuropsychiatric symptoms or confusion
diagnosisOverview: Citrullinemia type I is most commonly detected through expanded newborn screening: elevated plasma citrulline (usually above 100 μmol/L; reference < 35 μmol/L) on tandem mass spectrometry dried blood spot is the screening marker. Confirmatory testing includes plasma amino acid analysis demonstrating markedly elevated citrulline, reduced or absent argininosuccinate, elevated glutamine and ammonia, and reduced arginine. Enzyme activity measurement in fibroblasts or red blood cells and ASS1 gene sequencing confirm the diagnosis and define mutation severity. For type II, neonatal presentation is recognised by cholestasis, elevated citrulline, and galactosuria on newborn screening or clinical investigation; SLC25A13 gene sequencing confirms the diagnosis. In adults presenting with episodic hyperammonemia, plasma citrulline elevation plus SLC25A13 mutation analysis establishes CTLN2. Liver biopsy showing absent citrin immunostaining provides histological confirmation when genetic testing is inconclusive. Brain MRI in CTLN1 patients with prior hyperammonemic injury typically shows diffuse cerebral oedema acutely and white matter and basal ganglia signal changes in the recovery phase.

diagnosticTests.test: Newborn blood spot screening (tandem mass spectrometry)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects elevated citrulline on the dried blood spot, the primary newborn screening marker for CTLN1 and for NICCD; allows pre-symptomatic treatment initiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A heel-prick sample taken 24–72 hours after birth. Positive results require urgent same-day confirmatory plasma amino acid measurement and ammonia testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma amino acid analysis (citrulline, ammonia, arginine, glutamine)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms citrullinemia by quantifying markedly elevated citrulline (often > 1,000 μmol/L in severe CTLN1) and the full amino acid disturbance; guides nitrogen scavenger and arginine replacement dosing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood sample sent urgently when hyperammonemia is suspected; results take 3–7 days from a specialist metabolic laboratory but critical values are telephoned immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the severity of hyperammonemia; guides urgency of treatment; serial monitoring is essential during acute episodes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood collected in an ice-cold tube and processed within 15 minutes. Values above 200 μmol/L in a neonate require immediate intensive care management.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides definitive genetic confirmation for either type, distinguishes type I from type II, enables carrier testing of family members, and allows prenatal diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sent for next-generation sequencing; results take 2–6 weeks. In urgent neonatal situations, rapid sequencing panels covering all urea cycle genes are available at specialist centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated orotic acid confirms a urea cycle block; elevated levels in citrullinemia help differentiate it from conditions outside the urea cycle and support the diagnosis before genetic results return.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A spot or 24-hour urine sample; quantitative organic acid analysis by GC-MS takes 3–7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver biopsy with citrin immunostaining (for CTLN2 diagnosis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Histological absence of citrin protein on immunostaining confirms SLC25A13 deficiency when genetic testing is inconclusive or unavailable; also assesses the degree of liver fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A percutaneous liver biopsy under ultrasound guidance, typically performed under sedation or light general anaesthesia; the sample is sent to a specialist pathology laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses acute cerebral oedema during hyperammonemic crisis and documents permanent white matter or basal ganglia injury for neurodevelopmental prognostication.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30–60 minutes; neonates and young children require sedation. No ionising radiation is involved.

treatmentOverview: Emergency management of acute neonatal hyperammonemia in CTLN1 is the immediate priority: intravenous sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate (or arginine hydrochloride as a third nitrogen scavenger) at loading doses, combined with high glucose and lipid infusion to halt catabolism, can reduce ammonia by 50–70% within hours. If ammonia does not fall to safe levels within 4–8 hours of medical therapy (below 200 μmol/L in neonates), continuous veno-venous haemofiltration (CVVH) provides extracorporeal ammonia clearance and is the most rapid intervention available. Long-term management of CTLN1 combines natural protein restriction with a citrulline-free or low-citrulline essential amino acid formula, ongoing nitrogen scavenger therapy, oral arginine supplementation (because arginine is conditionally essential in ASS1 deficiency), and emergency protocols during illness. Liver transplantation definitively corrects ASS1 deficiency, normalising plasma citrulline and ammonia permanently, and is performed when metabolic control is inadequate or when recurrent crises cause progressive neurological damage. Management of citrullinemia type II differs substantially: CTLN2 patients respond poorly to conventional nitrogen scavengers and a high-carbohydrate diet can precipitate crises. Treatment emphasises a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate diet, avoidance of fructose and galactose, and supplementation with sodium pyruvate and medium-chain triglycerides to improve the cytosolic NADH imbalance. Liver transplantation is the definitive treatment for adult-onset CTLN2 and typically resolves the metabolic and neuropsychiatric manifestations completely.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate)
medicalTreatments.description: Loading doses of sodium benzoate (250 mg/kg) and sodium phenylacetate (250 mg/kg) followed by continuous infusion are given intravenously in neonatal hyperammonemic crisis to divert nitrogen excretion away from the blocked urea cycle. Sodium phenylacetate conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine; sodium benzoate conjugates with glycine to form hippurate; both are excreted in urine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In neonatal urea cycle disorder crises, intravenous nitrogen scavengers reduce plasma ammonia by approximately 50–70% within 6–12 hours when used alongside anabolic infusion; response rate exceeds 80% when ammonia is below 1,500 μmol/L at initiation.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral nitrogen scavengers (sodium phenylbutyrate or glycerol phenylbutyrate)
medicalTreatments.description: Maintenance therapy for long-term nitrogen scavenging in stable CTLN1 patients; glycerol phenylbutyrate (Ravicti) is better tolerated with lower sodium burden and more predictable absorption. Dosed every 6–8 hours with meals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show phenylbutyrate reduces plasma ammonia by approximately 30–50% versus baseline in urea cycle disorders; long-term use reduces hospitalisation rate for hyperammonemic crises by approximately 60%.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral arginine supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Arginine is conditionally essential in ASS1 deficiency because it cannot be synthesised from citrulline. Supplementation at 200–400 mg/kg/day ensures adequate arginine for protein synthesis, immune function, and nitric oxide production. It also provides a substrate to recycle nitrogen through residual cycle activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Arginine supplementation normalises plasma arginine in essentially 100% of CTLN1 patients when dosed appropriately; it also moderately reduces ammonia by enabling partial cycle completion through alternative ornithine sources.

medicalTreatments.name: Natural protein restriction with amino acid formula
medicalTreatments.description: Restricting natural protein limits nitrogenous waste and citrulline production; an essential amino acid formula without citrulline meets protein needs. Strict dietary adherence is required, particularly in the first years of life during rapid brain development.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined dietary restriction and nitrogen scavengers maintain normal ammonia in approximately 60–70% of CTLN1 patients on optimised therapy; outcomes are substantially better when started before the first hyperammonemic crisis.

medicalTreatments.name: Continuous veno-venous haemofiltration (CVVH)
medicalTreatments.description: Used as emergency extracorporeal therapy when medical management fails to reduce ammonia quickly enough, particularly in neonates with ammonia above 500–1,000 μmol/L. CVVH clears ammonia 10–30 times more efficiently than peritoneal dialysis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CVVH reduces ammonia to safe levels (below 200 μmol/L) within 4–8 hours in approximately 85–90% of neonatal cases and is the standard of care for refractory neonatal hyperammonemia in specialist centres.

medicalTreatments.name: High-fat, high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet (CTLN2)
medicalTreatments.description: The metabolic management of citrullinemia type II is opposite to type I: high-carbohydrate feeds worsen the cytosolic NADH imbalance. A high-protein, fat-rich diet with restricted carbohydrate, supplemented by medium-chain triglycerides and sodium pyruvate, corrects the hepatic redox imbalance and maintains acceptable ammonia levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dietary optimisation reduces hyperammonemic episodes in CTLN2 by approximately 50–70% in reported case series; most adult CTLN2 patients can maintain stability with diet alone for years before liver transplantation becomes necessary.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive treatment for both CTLN1 and CTLN2. The transplanted liver provides normal ASS1 (for CTLN1) or normal citrin (for CTLN2), completely correcting the underlying metabolic defect. For CTLN1, it is considered when recurrent crises cause progressive neurological injury or when biochemical control is impossible. For CTLN2, liver transplantation is the standard of care in adults with episodic encephalopathy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year graft survival after liver transplantation for urea cycle disorders exceeds 85%; plasma citrulline and ammonia normalise within days of transplant; CTLN2 neuropsychiatric symptoms resolve in the majority of transplanted patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sodium pyruvate supplementation (CTLN2)
alternativeTreatments.description: Sodium pyruvate is converted to oxaloacetate in mitochondria, accepting NADH and helping to re-oxidise the excess cytosolic NADH that accumulates in citrin deficiency. It is an adjunct to dietary management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oil (CTLN2)
alternativeTreatments.description: MCT provides energy via fatty acid beta-oxidation without burdening the cytosolic NADH pathway, making it a preferred fat source in CTLN2 dietary management alongside restriction of regular dietary fat in infants with NICCD.

whySeeSpecialist: Citrullinemia requires immediate specialist involvement at diagnosis because the neonatal hyperammonemic crisis is rapidly fatal without expert intervention. Long-term management — comprising protein prescription, nitrogen scavenger dosing, arginine supplementation, emergency protocols, and transplant planning — demands a metabolic physician with urea cycle disorder experience. The neurological sequelae of ammonia exposure require concurrent paediatric neurology input, and transplant centres with metabolic disease expertise are needed for surgical planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute management, any hospital must immediately contact a metabolic disease specialist by telephone before transferring a neonate with suspected citrullinemia; do not wait for transfer to begin intravenous nitrogen scavengers. For long-term care, seek a centre with a dedicated urea cycle disorder programme, a specialist metabolic dietitian experienced in low-protein diets, and access to liver transplant services. For CTLN2, a hepatologist experienced with metabolic liver disease and citrin deficiency is essential alongside the metabolic physician.
preventionStrategies: Expanded newborn screening using tandem mass spectrometry to detect citrulline elevation allows CTLN1 to be diagnosed before the neonatal crisis, enabling treatment to begin immediately.
Genetic carrier screening for couples with a family history of citrullinemia or SLC25A13 founder mutations in East Asian populations; prenatal diagnosis by gene analysis or enzyme assay is available.
Strict adherence to nitrogen scavenger therapy and the prescribed protein-restricted diet minimises crisis frequency in CTLN1.
Avoidance of carbohydrate-heavy meals and fasting prevents metabolic decompensation in CTLN2.
All family members (parents and siblings) should undergo carrier testing and plasma amino acid measurement at diagnosis.
lifestyleModifications: Take nitrogen scavenger medications every 6–8 hours with meals; never skip doses even when feeling well.
Strictly follow the prescribed dietary protein allowance and use the amino acid formula at every meal.
For CTLN2: eat small, frequent protein-rich meals; avoid sugar-sweetened drinks, large portions of rice, bread, or pasta, and prolonged fasting.
Carry an emergency protocol letter to any emergency department; present it immediately if vomiting, confusion, or seizures develop.
Stay well hydrated; avoid dehydration, which concentrates ammonia and worsens metabolic burden.
Inform treating physicians, dentists, and anaesthetists of the diagnosis before any procedure requiring fasting or sedation.

recommended: For CTLN1: low-protein natural foods supplemented with a citrulline-free essential amino acid formula to meet protein needs
For CTLN2: high-protein, fat-rich foods (meat, fish, eggs, tofu, dairy) to support aspartate generation and improve hepatic redox balance
Medium-chain triglyceride oil for infants with NICCD and as an energy supplement in CTLN2
Adequate carbohydrate for CTLN1 to prevent catabolism; restricted carbohydrate for CTLN2 to avoid worsening NADH imbalance
avoid: For CTLN1: high-protein foods beyond the prescribed natural protein allowance; foods with added citrulline
For CTLN2: high-carbohydrate meals, sugar, alcohol, and fructose or galactose-containing foods, which worsen the hepatic NADH burden
Prolonged fasting in both types, which triggers catabolism and nitrogen release

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is safe and encouraged for stable citrullinemia patients. Vigorous prolonged exercise increases muscle protein catabolism and can elevate ammonia; patients should eat a small carbohydrate-with-protein snack (CTLN1) or protein-rich snack (CTLN2) before exercise and stay well hydrated. Any episode of post-exercise confusion, vomiting, or unusual lethargy should be treated as a potential hyperammonemic episode and assessed urgently.
dailyManagement: Administer nitrogen scavenger medications (sodium phenylbutyrate or glycerol phenylbutyrate) at every meal as prescribed.
Measure and record natural protein intake against the dietitian's allowance daily; weigh amino acid formula doses accurately.
Keep a supply of emergency glucose polymer (for CTLN1) or protein foods (for CTLN2) at home for the first line of home management during early decompensation.
Monitor plasma ammonia and citrulline every 1–3 months when stable; more frequently after dose changes or illness.
Attend regular neurodevelopmental assessments in childhood; academic difficulties should trigger early educational support.
Contact the metabolic team immediately if the patient cannot tolerate medications or formula due to vomiting — do not wait more than 12 hours.
prognosis: Prognosis in citrullinemia type I depends primarily on whether the first hyperammonemic crisis is prevented or reversed before significant neurological damage occurs. Patients diagnosed through newborn screening who begin nitrogen scavenger therapy before their first crisis have a substantially better developmental outcome than those diagnosed after a clinical crisis; IQ scores and motor development approach normal in optimally treated patients. Each hyperammonemic episode with ammonia above 500 μmol/L carries a risk of permanent neurological injury; severity correlates with peak ammonia level and duration of exposure. Liver transplantation permanently resolves the metabolic defect and is associated with very good survival and quality of life. For citrullinemia type II, NICCD in infants typically resolves spontaneously by age 1 year in the majority; adult-onset CTLN2 is well managed with diet and, when necessary, liver transplantation, with transplanted patients typically experiencing full resolution of neuropsychiatric episodes. Life expectancy with treated citrullinemia approaches that of matched unaffected individuals in transplanted patients.
recoveryTimeline: Ammonia levels typically fall to safe ranges within 8–24 hours of initiating intravenous nitrogen scavengers and anabolic infusion in an acute crisis. Plasma citrulline normalises within days to weeks of starting dietary restriction. Neurological recovery from a hyperammonemic crisis depends on severity: mild episodes resolve fully within days; severe crises with prolonged high ammonia may leave persistent deficits that improve over months but may not fully resolve. After liver transplantation, plasma ammonia and citrulline normalise within 24–48 hours; neuropsychiatric CTLN2 symptoms resolve over weeks.
complications: Permanent neurological damage — intellectual disability, spastic diplegia, seizures, and cortical blindness — from acute hyperammonemic crises, especially in the neonatal period.
Cerebral oedema and herniation causing death during a severe untreated neonatal hyperammonemic crisis.
Liver cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma in adults with long-standing citrin deficiency and CTLN2.
Recurrent episodic encephalopathy causing progressive cognitive decline in adults with CTLN2 inadequately managed by diet alone.
Growth failure and chronic undernutrition from the demands of long-term protein restriction in CTLN1.
Psychological and psychiatric burden: anxiety, depression, and OCD are elevated in children and adults living with frequent crisis risk and demanding dietary regimens.

supportResources.name: National Urea Cycle Disorders Foundation (NUCDF)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organisation for all urea cycle disorders including citrullinemia, providing education, family support, and research funding.

supportResources.name: NORD — Citrullinemia Overview
supportResources.description: Plain-language disease overview of citrullinemia from the National Organization for Rare Disorders.

supportResources.name: Citrin Foundation
supportResources.description: International foundation dedicated to citrin deficiency (CTLN2 and NICCD) research, patient support, and physician education.

sources.title: Häberle J et al. Suggested guidelines for the diagnosis and management of urea cycle disorders. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases, 2012
Tuchman M et al. Prospective versus treatment outcomes in a multicenter study of urea cycle disorders. Molecular Genetics and Metabolism, 2008

confusedWithConditions.name: Argininemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Argininemia causes elevated arginine, not citrulline, and presents as progressive spastic diplegia in childhood rather than neonatal hyperammonemia; ammonia is mildly elevated compared with the profound hyperammonemia of neonatal citrullinemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other urea cycle disorders
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OTC deficiency and CPS1 deficiency cause similar neonatal hyperammonemia but have very low plasma citrulline rather than the markedly elevated citrulline characteristic of ASS1 deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Galactosemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Galactosemia also causes neonatal jaundice, liver failure, and can elevate citrulline secondarily, but is distinguished by elevated galactose-1-phosphate, positive reducing substances in urine, and deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase.

relatedConditions.name: Argininemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another urea cycle disorder managed at the same specialist centres with shared nitrogen scavenger treatment principles, though presenting differently.

relatedConditions.name: Urea cycle disorders
relatedConditions.relevance: Citrullinemia is one of the five classical urea cycle disorders; all involve overlapping management strategies including nitrogen scavengers and dietary protein restriction.

relatedConditions.name: Propionic acidemia
relatedConditions.relevance: An organic acid disorder causing secondary hyperammonemia in infancy; managed with similar emergency protocols and specialist metabolic follow-up.

relatedConditions.name: Galactosemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inborn error of metabolism causing neonatal liver disease and detected on newborn screening; important differential diagnosis for neonatal NICCD citrullinemia type II.

faqs.question: What is citrullinemia?
faqs.answer: Citrullinemia is an inherited metabolic disorder in which the amino acid citrulline accumulates to toxic levels because a urea cycle enzyme is deficient. Type I is caused by ASS1 enzyme deficiency and causes severe hyperammonemia in newborns. Type II is caused by citrin deficiency and can cause liver disease in infancy or episodic neuropsychiatric illness in adults.

faqs.question: How serious is citrullinemia in newborns?
faqs.answer: Neonatal citrullinemia type I is a medical emergency. Without treatment within the first days of life, plasma ammonia rises above 1,000 μmol/L, causing seizures, coma, and death. With immediate nitrogen scavenger therapy and dietary management, most affected neonates survive but may have lasting neurological effects.

faqs.question: How is citrullinemia detected at birth?
faqs.answer: Expanded newborn screening using tandem mass spectrometry detects elevated citrulline on the dried blood spot taken in the first 24–72 hours of life. A positive screen triggers urgent plasma amino acid and ammonia testing. Early detection allows treatment to begin before the neonatal crisis develops.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for citrullinemia type I?
faqs.answer: Acute treatment uses intravenous nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate and sodium phenylacetate) plus glucose and lipid infusion to halt catabolism. Long-term treatment combines natural protein restriction, a citrulline-free amino acid formula, oral nitrogen scavengers, oral arginine supplementation, and emergency protocols during illness. Liver transplantation is curative.

faqs.question: What is citrullinemia type II?
faqs.answer: Citrullinemia type II (CTLN2) is caused by citrin deficiency due to SLC25A13 mutations. It mainly affects East Asian populations. In infancy it can cause neonatal jaundice (NICCD). In adults it causes episodic confusion, unusual behaviour, and sometimes liver cirrhosis. It is managed with a high-protein, low-carbohydrate diet and liver transplantation.

faqs.question: Can citrullinemia type II be confused with psychiatric illness?
faqs.answer: Yes. The episodic confusion, mania, aggression, and personality changes of adult-onset CTLN2 are frequently misdiagnosed as psychiatric disorders including bipolar disorder or schizophrenia. The characteristic craving for protein-rich foods and aversion to carbohydrates can be an important clue. Plasma ammonia and amino acid measurement resolves the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is liver transplantation necessary for citrullinemia?
faqs.answer: Liver transplantation provides the liver with a normal copy of ASS1 (type I) or citrin (type II) and is curative for the metabolic defect. It is considered for type I patients who cannot achieve biochemical control or have recurrent crises, and is standard treatment for adults with symptomatic CTLN2.

faqs.question: What happens to the brain during a hyperammonemic crisis?
faqs.answer: High ammonia crosses the blood-brain barrier and is converted to glutamine in astrocytes, causing cell swelling and cerebral oedema. Prolonged high ammonia disrupts neurotransmitter balance and mitochondrial function. In severe neonatal crises, cerebral oedema can progress to herniation. Survivors of severe crises can have intellectual disability, epilepsy, or spasticity.

faqs.question: Why does citrullinemia type II worsen after carbohydrate meals?
faqs.answer: Citrin deficiency impairs the malate-aspartate shuttle, causing excess NADH to accumulate in hepatocytes. Carbohydrate metabolism generates additional cytosolic NADH, worsening the redox imbalance and impairing the urea cycle further. This is why CTLN2 patients typically prefer protein and fat over carbohydrates — a metabolically adaptive food preference.

faqs.question: How is arginine involved in citrullinemia treatment?
faqs.answer: In type I citrullinemia, arginine cannot be synthesised normally from citrulline because the cycle is blocked. Arginine supplementation restores this essential amino acid and allows any residual cycle activity to function, modestly reducing ammonia. It is given alongside nitrogen scavengers and dietary restriction.

faqs.question: What is the role of haemodialysis in citrullinemia?
faqs.answer: Continuous veno-venous haemofiltration (CVVH) is used as emergency extracorporeal therapy when medication alone cannot reduce ammonia quickly enough. It is far more efficient than peritoneal dialysis for ammonia removal and can lower ammonia to safe levels within 4–8 hours in severe neonatal cases.

faqs.question: Can adults be diagnosed with citrullinemia for the first time?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly with citrullinemia type II. Adults of East Asian descent may have their first episode of hyperammonemic encephalopathy at age 20–50 years, sometimes after a precipitating dietary change or illness. The diagnosis requires plasma amino acid analysis showing elevated citrulline and SLC25A13 gene testing.

faqs.question: Is citrullinemia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Both types are autosomal recessive: a child must inherit one mutant gene from each parent to be affected. Parents are healthy carriers. Each pregnancy of two carriers has a 25% chance of producing an affected child. Genetic carrier screening is available for families and at-risk ethnic groups.

faqs.question: What is NICCD?
faqs.answer: NICCD stands for neonatal intrahepatic cholestasis caused by citrin deficiency. It is the infantile form of citrin deficiency (SLC25A13 mutations), presenting as prolonged jaundice, fatty liver, and elevated bilirubin in the first months of life. Most affected infants recover liver function by age 1, but some progress to adult-onset CTLN2.

faqs.question: How is citrullinemia monitored long-term?
faqs.answer: Plasma citrulline, arginine, ammonia, and amino acid profile are measured every 1–3 months in stable patients. Liver function tests, growth, nutritional status, and neurodevelopment are assessed at regular specialist visits. Frequency increases during illness, dietary changes, and around surgical procedures.

faqs.question: What are nitrogen scavengers?
faqs.answer: Nitrogen scavengers are medications — sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate, and sodium phenylbutyrate — that provide alternative pathways for excreting nitrogen when the urea cycle is blocked. Sodium benzoate combines with glycine to form hippurate; phenylacetate combines with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine; both are then excreted in the urine, reducing ammonia buildup.

faqs.question: Can someone with citrullinemia have a normal life?
faqs.answer: Children with CTLN1 who are identified by newborn screening and treated before their first crisis can develop normally and attend regular school. Adults with CTLN2 who undergo liver transplantation typically regain normal cognitive function and quality of life. Long-term specialist follow-up and dietary adherence are required throughout life.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis if citrullinemia is untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated neonatal citrullinemia type I leads to death or severe permanent brain damage within the first week of life. Untreated adult CTLN2 causes progressive encephalopathy with each crisis, ultimately resulting in severe dementia, cirrhosis, or liver failure.

faqs.question: Are there clinical trials for citrullinemia?
faqs.answer: Research into mRNA therapy and gene editing approaches to restore ASS1 or citrin function is ongoing in preclinical models. Improved nitrogen scavenger formulations and nutritional interventions are also being studied. Patients can search clinicaltrials.gov using 'citrullinemia' or 'ASS1 deficiency' to find recruiting studies.

faqs.question: Does citrullinemia affect females differently than males?
faqs.answer: Both sexes are equally affected by both forms. Pregnancy poses additional risk for women with CTLN1, as it increases metabolic demands; specialist obstetric-metabolic co-management is needed throughout. CTLN2 does not appear to have a sex-specific difference in severity or presentation.

metaDescription: Citrullinemia is a urea cycle disorder causing ammonia buildup in newborns or episodic encephalopathy in adults. Learn types, treatment, and prognosis.
